item.title: Shrek Rave
item.description:
Saturday, March 14, 2026 at 10 PM

Location: The Crocodile, 2505 1st Ave, Seattle, WA

Its time to raise your ogre light sticks for a spectacular night of bombastic music thatll take over the Shrek Rave! Its gonna be wild, whimsical, and unforgettable as a crowd of ogre-loving beasts comes together to celebrate the iconic films music, culture, and all-out randomness. A crazy-viral night that soon took over cities outside of the U.S., people have turned up dressed as Shrek, Fiona, Donkey, and many more of its quirky characters. Of course, the highlight of the night is the dance floor! All the hits such as Im a Believer, Bad Reputation, I Need a Hero, Accidentally in Love, and many more will have you dancing and letting it all out. Pretty much a cultural marvel by now, its clear why a huge dance floor is gonna pop up at The Crocodile for Shrek Rave on Saturday, March 14th. Dont miss out by booking your tickets now! This event is 21 and over. Any ticket holder unable to present valid identification indicating that they are at least 21 years of age will not be admitted to this event, and will not be eligible for a refund. All sales are final. There are no refunds unless the event is cancelled or postponed Opening Acts are subject to change or cancelation at any time without notice. No refund will be owed if an Opening Act is changed or canceled. Doors times subject to change Tickets may not be resold or offered for resale. If we suspect tickets have been purchased for resale purposes only, we reserve the right to void or cancel the tickets without refund. Tickets obtained from unauthorized sources may be invalid, lost, stolen or counterfeit and if so, are void. Price: $36.36 - $36.36

From The Crocodile

item.title: Beyond Mysticism: The Modern Northwest
item.description:
Sunday, March 15, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Seattle Art Museum, 1300 First Avenue, Seattle, WA

image: Drift No. 2, 1936, Malcolm M. Roberts, American, 1913-1990, tempera on board, 25 1/2 x 20 5/8 in., Seattle Art Museum, Eugene Fuller Memorial Collection, 37.103, © Malcolm M. Roberts, photo: Scott Leen. Inspired by the region’s natural beauty and rapidly changing city, this exhibition showcases artists who told the raw story of Seattle in the 20th century. By sharing the Pacific Northwest’s perspective through trending art styles—including Social Realism, Surrealism, and Abstract Expressionism—these artists offered a unique take on Modernist art and challenged the “mystic” label ascribed to their works. Spearheading the movement were local icons Mark Tobey, Kenneth Callahan, Guy Anderson, and Morris Graves—but the range and breadth of Pacific Northwest art goes beyond the “Big Four” of the Northwest School. Featuring more than 150 pieces, this exhibition showcases works that investigate the complexities of daily life in the region. Whether applying an ecocritical lens or producing dreamy and uncanny visuals, these artists expressed the duality of the natural world and city life to warn of the environmental and social impacts of industrialization. From Malcolm Roberts to Kenjiro Nomura, learn how Northwest artists applied their regional perspective to broader artistic developments to create a new form of American Modernism. The exhibition is accompanied by a fully illustrated catalogue authored by Theresa Papanikolas, SAM’s Ann M. Barwick Curator of American Art.

From SEAtoday - Arts & Culture

Event details

item.title: TADAIMA: I'm Home
item.description:
Sunday, March 15, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Museum of History and Industry, 860 Terry Ave N, Seattle, Seattle, WA

After more than 80 years, a treasured collection returns “home” to Seattle’s Japanese American community. In 1942 dozens of traditional Boys’ and Girls’ Day dolls were entrusted to Bailey Gatzert Elementary School by Japanese American families facing forced incarceration. Most were never reclaimed after the war. Artist Miya Sukune uses recent research in MOHAI’s collection, as well as interviews with Japanese American survivors and their descendants, to create an installation called “Tadaima,” meaning “I’m home.” TADAIMA: “I’m Home!” Bringing Seattle’s Girls’ and Boys’ Day Dolls Home reconnects the dolls with their traditional role of protecting and blessing children. It invites visitors to celebrate the resilience, memory, and enduring traditions of the Japanese American Community.

From SEAtoday - Arts & Culture

Event details

item.title: Samantha Yun Wall: What We Leave Behind
item.description:
Sunday, March 15, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Seattle Art Museum, 1300 First Avenue, Seattle, WA

Samantha Yun Wall’s new paintings use overlapping silhouettes of female figures as portals to unknown spaces and different temporal realms. Impetus for the new body of work is a Korean folk tale in which the Pasque flower is symbolic of a grandmother who passed away without the loving care of her grandchildren. It is a story of melancholy, loss, and remembrance. The delicate hair on the flower’s stem differentiates it, and the artist gives the plant a surreal aspect in some of her paintings, replacing the flower’s center with a watchful eye.

From SEAtoday - Arts & Culture

Event details

item.title: Anila Quayyum Agha: Geometry of Light
item.description:
Sunday, March 15, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Seattle Art Museum, 1300 First Avenue, Seattle, WA

Anila Quayyum Agha: Geometry of Light invites you to become part of the art. Agha, a Pakistani American artist, animates spaces with her large-scale sculptural installations. Suspended from the ceiling, Agha’s steel cubes are laser-cut with intricate designs that project geometric shadows onto the visitor. She draws on both the light and dark of her own life, using South Asian art practices to convey the gender discrimination she faced growing up as a young girl in Pakistan. An in-depth exploration of Islamic architecture, art, and identity, this is the first solo exhibition of a Pakistani American artist in SAM’s 90-year history. Agha is carving a name out for herself in the art world—visit the Seattle Asian Art Museum for an immersive and illuminating experience.

From SEAtoday - Arts & Culture

Event details

Event details

item.title: Young Dragon: A Bruce Lee Story
item.description:
Sunday, March 15, 2026 at 11 AM

Location: Seattle Children's Theatre, 201 Thomas Street, Seattle, WA

Before he was a legend, Bruce Lee was just a young man trying to figure it all out—right here in Seattle. This action-packed, soul-searching story follows the Young Dragon as he navigates a new country and finds his voice. Through movement, magic, and heart, we meet the human behind the icon—flawed, fierce, and full of possibility. Watch as Lee learns to clear his brain by mimicking the white noise of water and begins fusing martial arts with philosophy. From street fights in Hong Kong to teaching self-defense and chasing big dreams, Bruce’s journey reminds us that finding your way means first figuring out who you are. Come see how Seattle shaped him into a flexible, fluid, and flowing master—and how he reshaped the world.

From SEAtoday - Arts & Culture

Event details

item.title: Boren Banner Series: Camille Trautman
item.description:
Sunday, March 15, 2026 at 11 AM

Location: Frye Art Museum, 704 Terry Ave, Seattle, WA

Every photograph is a reminder that the act of framing is never neutral. In Camille Trautman’s first solo museum exhibition in their hometown, the Seattle-born Duwamish artist uses photography and video to challenge colonial narratives and counter Indigenous erasure. The exhibition presents selections from their ongoing series The North American LCD—spectral self-portraits staged in varied natural landscapes. Rooted in Trautman’s process of coming out as transgender, the images show the artist’s body partially obscured by large LCD screens. The series examines how both landscape photography and digital media can shape or distort cultural identity, often by promoting or denying visibility.

From SEAtoday - Arts & Culture

item.title: TADAIMA: I'm Home
item.description:
Monday, March 16, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Museum of History and Industry, 860 Terry Ave N, Seattle, Seattle, WA

After more than 80 years, a treasured collection returns “home” to Seattle’s Japanese American community. In 1942 dozens of traditional Boys’ and Girls’ Day dolls were entrusted to Bailey Gatzert Elementary School by Japanese American families facing forced incarceration. Most were never reclaimed after the war. Artist Miya Sukune uses recent research in MOHAI’s collection, as well as interviews with Japanese American survivors and their descendants, to create an installation called “Tadaima,” meaning “I’m home.” TADAIMA: “I’m Home!” Bringing Seattle’s Girls’ and Boys’ Day Dolls Home reconnects the dolls with their traditional role of protecting and blessing children. It invites visitors to celebrate the resilience, memory, and enduring traditions of the Japanese American Community.

From SEAtoday - Arts & Culture

item.title: Susan Meiselas: Crossings
item.description:
Monday, March 16, 2026 at 12 PM

Location: Photographic Center Northwest (PCNW), 900 12th Ave., Seattle, WA

First presented at the Art Institute of Chicago in 1990, this exhibition by Susan Meiselas intertwines documentary photographs of Central America during the 1970s and 1980s \(from Nicaragua and El Salvador\) along with panoramic images from the US/Mexico border taken in 1989. Additional photographs of the US/Mexico border taken in 2018 are also featured in a site-specific gallery installation. The exhibition invites viewers to see immigration in the context of economic and political crises in the region. Today, the work feels newly urgent. US immigration enforcement has expanded through militarization and constant surveillance, with leaders using fear-driven language to divide communities and legitimize brutal forms of control. These photographs ask us to look carefully, to recognize familiar patterns, and to hold on to our shared humanity across the globe. People move in search of safety, possibility, and a better life for their families, which is a universal hope that crosses borders, generations, and time.

From SEAtoday - Arts & Culture

item.title: Issaquah Drink N' Draw
item.description:
Monday, March 16, 2026 at 6 PM

Location: Formula Brewing, 1875 NW Poplar Way, Issaquah, WA

ISSAQUAH'S LOCAL ART MEETUP Calling all artists and art enthusiasts! Join us for an evening filled with creativity, camaraderie, and, of course, drinks at your local Issaquah Drink n' Draw! Location: Formula Brewing, 1875 NW Poplar Way, Issaquah, WA 98027 Indulge your artistic side in a relaxed and social setting. Whether you're a seasoned artist or just starting your creative journey, this event is the perfect opportunity to: Draw: Bring your sketchbooks, pencils, tablets, or any medium of your choice. As long as it can fit in a 2' x 2' space and can be cleaned up easily, we welcome art of all types! Unleash your imagination and create alongside like-minded individuals. Let your creativity flow! Don't know what to draw? Search the hashtag \#essdrawmonthly or find the latest pinned Prompt List on the ESS group page! ️ Chat: Engage in lively conversations with fellow artists. Share tips, techniques, and stories while getting inspired by each other's work. Connect with a diverse community passionate about art. Drink: Sip on your favorite beverages from Formula's selection of refreshments. Whether it's a cold beer, a glass of wine, or a non-alcoholic option, enjoy a drink while you immerse yourself in the creative process. ✏️ This event is open to artists of all skill levels and backgrounds. Feel free to join us solo or invite friends to share in this artistic experience. Admission is FREE. Seating is first-come, first-served. While there's usually enough table space, if we get too busy, you may have to spread out. Come and be a part of this vibrant artistic gathering! Let's sketch, chat, and create memorable art together. We can't wait to see your masterpieces! For more information, contact group organizers Blayne Fox and Kat Klockow. Note: Remember to drink responsibly and be mindful of others' space and creativity during the event.

From SEAtoday - Arts & Culture

item.title: EMBODIED ALTARS DANCE CLASS
item.description:
Monday, March 16, 2026 at 6:30 PM

Location: Daybreak Star Indian Cultural Center, 5011 Bernie Whitebear Way, Seattle, WA

EMBODIED ALTARS is an all-levels dance class hosted by Daybreak Star Radio. Designed by Indigenous Chicana artist and El Sueño director, Alicia Mullikin. Dancers will utilize Indigenous principles within movement, meditation, and song as a way to practice gratitude, honor the land, and recognize their ancestors. The class is for those who are interested in reflection, intention setting, and a discovery of their own stories. No dance experience necessary. Class Time: Mondays, 6:30-8PM No class 9/15/25 No class 10/13/25 Venue: Daybreak Star Indian Cultural Center 5011 Bernie Whitebear Way, Seattle, WA 98199 Sliding Scale: $10-$20\* per class. Sign Up Now!   This venue is ADA accessible w/ ADA bathrooms and a wheelchair lift to the second floor. Class will be held in the ballroom space, this area has 5 ADA parking spaces. There are an additional 25 parking spots in our lot, there is ample parking space along the road leading up to Daybreak star as well as the Discovery Park North Parking lot. What to bring: water, comfortable clothes to dance in, journal, your personal identity, a desire to explore new pathways and an open mind/heart. Hosted at Daybreak Star Indian Cultural Center Produced by United Indians of All Tribes Foundation In Partnership with El Sueno Dance & Daybreak Star Radio “Indigenize the Airwaves” “Art. Culture. Reclamation.” \*Talk to us about BIPOC and Queer/LGBTQIA2S\+ Scholorships\* Please contact @el.sueno.dance on IG for private conversations about this topic.

From SEAtoday - Arts & Culture

item.title: Screen Printing Open Session
item.description:
Tuesday, March 17, 2026 at 4 PM

Location: 305 Harrison Street, Seattle, Washington 98109, United States

You MUST take the SP 101: Intro to Vera's Screen Print Studio class before you can sign up for an Open Session Vera’s screen print studio offers four-hour Open Sessions where individuals who have completed the introductory class may use our screen print lab to work on their own projects. Our Open Sessions are looked over by a volunteer moderator, who can provide you with advice and guidance for anything taught in our Screen Printing 101 class. We offer three different kinds of tickets at Open Sessions: Vacuum Table for paper and other flat media, T-shirt Press for shirts, tote bags and other fabrics, and Screen prep for just screen preparation. Each ticket is limited to two. If the ticket you are looking for is sold out, please add yourself to the waitlist so you can be notified when a ticket becomes available. For Open Sessions, Vera provides: - Rentable screens - Emulsion and emulsion remover - Squegees - Basic tools like spatulas, rulers, tape, etc. Participants must provide their own: - Substrate (paper, t-shirts, etc) - Water based ink Vera does not allow the use of non-water based inks at our studio, as our studio is not set up to safely process non-water based ink. If you have any questions, please email matt@theveraproject.org. FAQ 1\. Can Vera print a transparent stencil for me? Yes — You must send us your stencil at least 24 hours before the start of your session. Your file must be a .PDF, no larger than 12" x 18", and ready to print. Vera cannot make any edits to your file. Stencils are $5 each and can be paid via Debit/Credit when you arrive. If you are not interested in getting a stencil printed at Vera, you can print your own on transparency at a FedEx or similar print service location. 2\. What if I need direct assistance on my projects? Our moderators are trained to assist with any topics covered in the SP 101 class offered at Vera. While some may be able to give you basic advice on your project, they are not expected to ask as a direct instructor. If you have a project where you would like one-on-one guidance, please reach out to matt@theveraproject.org to request private tutoring. 3\. I really want to use your studio during an Open Session, but there are no slots available? Our studio is in high demand, so we apologize if our Open Session is sold out — We have limited space in the studio and several events every day at Vera, so it can be difficult for us to find more time for Open Sessions. If you're interested in booking the studio outside of Open Sessions, please email matt@theveraproject.org to discuss private rental rates and availability.

From The Vera Project

item.title: Beyond Mysticism: The Modern Northwest
item.description:
Wednesday, March 18, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Seattle Art Museum, 1300 First Avenue, Seattle, WA

image: Drift No. 2, 1936, Malcolm M. Roberts, American, 1913-1990, tempera on board, 25 1/2 x 20 5/8 in., Seattle Art Museum, Eugene Fuller Memorial Collection, 37.103, © Malcolm M. Roberts, photo: Scott Leen. Inspired by the region’s natural beauty and rapidly changing city, this exhibition showcases artists who told the raw story of Seattle in the 20th century. By sharing the Pacific Northwest’s perspective through trending art styles—including Social Realism, Surrealism, and Abstract Expressionism—these artists offered a unique take on Modernist art and challenged the “mystic” label ascribed to their works. Spearheading the movement were local icons Mark Tobey, Kenneth Callahan, Guy Anderson, and Morris Graves—but the range and breadth of Pacific Northwest art goes beyond the “Big Four” of the Northwest School. Featuring more than 150 pieces, this exhibition showcases works that investigate the complexities of daily life in the region. Whether applying an ecocritical lens or producing dreamy and uncanny visuals, these artists expressed the duality of the natural world and city life to warn of the environmental and social impacts of industrialization. From Malcolm Roberts to Kenjiro Nomura, learn how Northwest artists applied their regional perspective to broader artistic developments to create a new form of American Modernism. The exhibition is accompanied by a fully illustrated catalogue authored by Theresa Papanikolas, SAM’s Ann M. Barwick Curator of American Art.

From SEAtoday - Arts & Culture

Event details

item.title: TADAIMA: I'm Home
item.description:
Wednesday, March 18, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Museum of History and Industry, 860 Terry Ave N, Seattle, Seattle, WA

After more than 80 years, a treasured collection returns “home” to Seattle’s Japanese American community. In 1942 dozens of traditional Boys’ and Girls’ Day dolls were entrusted to Bailey Gatzert Elementary School by Japanese American families facing forced incarceration. Most were never reclaimed after the war. Artist Miya Sukune uses recent research in MOHAI’s collection, as well as interviews with Japanese American survivors and their descendants, to create an installation called “Tadaima,” meaning “I’m home.” TADAIMA: “I’m Home!” Bringing Seattle’s Girls’ and Boys’ Day Dolls Home reconnects the dolls with their traditional role of protecting and blessing children. It invites visitors to celebrate the resilience, memory, and enduring traditions of the Japanese American Community.

From SEAtoday - Arts & Culture

Event details

item.title: Samantha Yun Wall: What We Leave Behind
item.description:
Wednesday, March 18, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Seattle Art Museum, 1300 First Avenue, Seattle, WA

Samantha Yun Wall’s new paintings use overlapping silhouettes of female figures as portals to unknown spaces and different temporal realms. Impetus for the new body of work is a Korean folk tale in which the Pasque flower is symbolic of a grandmother who passed away without the loving care of her grandchildren. It is a story of melancholy, loss, and remembrance. The delicate hair on the flower’s stem differentiates it, and the artist gives the plant a surreal aspect in some of her paintings, replacing the flower’s center with a watchful eye.

From SEAtoday - Arts & Culture

Event details

item.title: Anila Quayyum Agha: Geometry of Light
item.description:
Wednesday, March 18, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Seattle Art Museum, 1300 First Avenue, Seattle, WA

Anila Quayyum Agha: Geometry of Light invites you to become part of the art. Agha, a Pakistani American artist, animates spaces with her large-scale sculptural installations. Suspended from the ceiling, Agha’s steel cubes are laser-cut with intricate designs that project geometric shadows onto the visitor. She draws on both the light and dark of her own life, using South Asian art practices to convey the gender discrimination she faced growing up as a young girl in Pakistan. An in-depth exploration of Islamic architecture, art, and identity, this is the first solo exhibition of a Pakistani American artist in SAM’s 90-year history. Agha is carving a name out for herself in the art world—visit the Seattle Asian Art Museum for an immersive and illuminating experience.

From SEAtoday - Arts & Culture

item.title: Boren Banner Series: Camille Trautman
item.description:
Wednesday, March 18, 2026 at 11 AM

Location: Frye Art Museum, 704 Terry Ave, Seattle, WA

Every photograph is a reminder that the act of framing is never neutral. In Camille Trautman’s first solo museum exhibition in their hometown, the Seattle-born Duwamish artist uses photography and video to challenge colonial narratives and counter Indigenous erasure. The exhibition presents selections from their ongoing series The North American LCD—spectral self-portraits staged in varied natural landscapes. Rooted in Trautman’s process of coming out as transgender, the images show the artist’s body partially obscured by large LCD screens. The series examines how both landscape photography and digital media can shape or distort cultural identity, often by promoting or denying visibility.

From SEAtoday - Arts & Culture

Event details

item.title: Susan Meiselas: Crossings
item.description:
Wednesday, March 18, 2026 at 12 PM

Location: Photographic Center Northwest (PCNW), 900 12th Ave., Seattle, WA

First presented at the Art Institute of Chicago in 1990, this exhibition by Susan Meiselas intertwines documentary photographs of Central America during the 1970s and 1980s \(from Nicaragua and El Salvador\) along with panoramic images from the US/Mexico border taken in 1989. Additional photographs of the US/Mexico border taken in 2018 are also featured in a site-specific gallery installation. The exhibition invites viewers to see immigration in the context of economic and political crises in the region. Today, the work feels newly urgent. US immigration enforcement has expanded through militarization and constant surveillance, with leaders using fear-driven language to divide communities and legitimize brutal forms of control. These photographs ask us to look carefully, to recognize familiar patterns, and to hold on to our shared humanity across the globe. People move in search of safety, possibility, and a better life for their families, which is a universal hope that crosses borders, generations, and time.

From SEAtoday - Arts & Culture

Guests are encouraged to bring new or gently used pet items, including blankets, leashes, collars, toys, and other pet supplies. item.title: Beyond Mysticism: The Modern Northwest
item.description:
Friday, March 20, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Seattle Art Museum, 1300 First Avenue, Seattle, WA

image: Drift No. 2, 1936, Malcolm M. Roberts, American, 1913-1990, tempera on board, 25 1/2 x 20 5/8 in., Seattle Art Museum, Eugene Fuller Memorial Collection, 37.103, © Malcolm M. Roberts, photo: Scott Leen. Inspired by the region’s natural beauty and rapidly changing city, this exhibition showcases artists who told the raw story of Seattle in the 20th century. By sharing the Pacific Northwest’s perspective through trending art styles—including Social Realism, Surrealism, and Abstract Expressionism—these artists offered a unique take on Modernist art and challenged the “mystic” label ascribed to their works. Spearheading the movement were local icons Mark Tobey, Kenneth Callahan, Guy Anderson, and Morris Graves—but the range and breadth of Pacific Northwest art goes beyond the “Big Four” of the Northwest School. Featuring more than 150 pieces, this exhibition showcases works that investigate the complexities of daily life in the region. Whether applying an ecocritical lens or producing dreamy and uncanny visuals, these artists expressed the duality of the natural world and city life to warn of the environmental and social impacts of industrialization. From Malcolm Roberts to Kenjiro Nomura, learn how Northwest artists applied their regional perspective to broader artistic developments to create a new form of American Modernism. The exhibition is accompanied by a fully illustrated catalogue authored by Theresa Papanikolas, SAM’s Ann M. Barwick Curator of American Art.

From SEAtoday - Arts & Culture

Event details

item.title: TADAIMA: I'm Home
item.description:
Friday, March 20, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Museum of History and Industry, 860 Terry Ave N, Seattle, Seattle, WA

After more than 80 years, a treasured collection returns “home” to Seattle’s Japanese American community. In 1942 dozens of traditional Boys’ and Girls’ Day dolls were entrusted to Bailey Gatzert Elementary School by Japanese American families facing forced incarceration. Most were never reclaimed after the war. Artist Miya Sukune uses recent research in MOHAI’s collection, as well as interviews with Japanese American survivors and their descendants, to create an installation called “Tadaima,” meaning “I’m home.” TADAIMA: “I’m Home!” Bringing Seattle’s Girls’ and Boys’ Day Dolls Home reconnects the dolls with their traditional role of protecting and blessing children. It invites visitors to celebrate the resilience, memory, and enduring traditions of the Japanese American Community.

From SEAtoday - Arts & Culture

Event details

item.title: Samantha Yun Wall: What We Leave Behind
item.description:
Friday, March 20, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Seattle Art Museum, 1300 First Avenue, Seattle, WA

Samantha Yun Wall’s new paintings use overlapping silhouettes of female figures as portals to unknown spaces and different temporal realms. Impetus for the new body of work is a Korean folk tale in which the Pasque flower is symbolic of a grandmother who passed away without the loving care of her grandchildren. It is a story of melancholy, loss, and remembrance. The delicate hair on the flower’s stem differentiates it, and the artist gives the plant a surreal aspect in some of her paintings, replacing the flower’s center with a watchful eye.

From SEAtoday - Arts & Culture

Event details

item.title: Anila Quayyum Agha: Geometry of Light
item.description:
Friday, March 20, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Seattle Art Museum, 1300 First Avenue, Seattle, WA

Anila Quayyum Agha: Geometry of Light invites you to become part of the art. Agha, a Pakistani American artist, animates spaces with her large-scale sculptural installations. Suspended from the ceiling, Agha’s steel cubes are laser-cut with intricate designs that project geometric shadows onto the visitor. She draws on both the light and dark of her own life, using South Asian art practices to convey the gender discrimination she faced growing up as a young girl in Pakistan. An in-depth exploration of Islamic architecture, art, and identity, this is the first solo exhibition of a Pakistani American artist in SAM’s 90-year history. Agha is carving a name out for herself in the art world—visit the Seattle Asian Art Museum for an immersive and illuminating experience.

From SEAtoday - Arts & Culture

Event details

item.title: Boren Banner Series: Camille Trautman
item.description:
Friday, March 20, 2026 at 11 AM

Location: Frye Art Museum, 704 Terry Ave, Seattle, WA

Every photograph is a reminder that the act of framing is never neutral. In Camille Trautman’s first solo museum exhibition in their hometown, the Seattle-born Duwamish artist uses photography and video to challenge colonial narratives and counter Indigenous erasure. The exhibition presents selections from their ongoing series The North American LCD—spectral self-portraits staged in varied natural landscapes. Rooted in Trautman’s process of coming out as transgender, the images show the artist’s body partially obscured by large LCD screens. The series examines how both landscape photography and digital media can shape or distort cultural identity, often by promoting or denying visibility.

From SEAtoday - Arts & Culture

item.title: The Taste of Iceland Festival March 19-21
item.description:
Friday, March 20, 2026 at 4 PM

Location: National Nordic Museum, 2655 NW Market Street, Seattle, WA

Taste of Iceland returns to Seattle from Thursday, March 19, through Saturday, March 21. The cultural festival celebrates Iceland with events across the city showcasing the best of Icelandic culture, including food, music, nature, art, travel, and more. Most events are free and open to the public, including two free concerts, a prix fixe Icelandic dining experience, a comedy show, and a cocktail class. Tickets are required and guests are encouraged to arrive promptly to guarantee entry. Tickets are available on the Taste of Iceland Seattle website.  Taste of Iceland events include: Thursday, March 19 - Saturday, March 21 Icelandic Menu at Palace Kitchen: Ready to taste a plate full of Iceland? Icelandic Chef Ísak Aron Jóhannsson and Palace Kitchen Chef Ron Anderson will prepare a feast inspired by Iceland’s natural beauty and local flavors, including cod from Icelandic Seafood and lamb from Icelandic Lamb. This pop-up dining experience is available each evening beginning at 5:00 PM at Palace Kitchen. Reservations for the prix fixe menu are available at https://www.inspiredbyiceland.com/taste-of-iceland/seattle/icelandic-menu. Wheel of Prizes Presented by Icelandair: Select guests will have the opportunity to spin the Wheel of Prizes for a chance to win amazing prizes from all festival partners during multiple events, including a trip for two to Iceland! Learn more at https://www.inspiredbyiceland.com/taste-of-iceland/seattle/wheel-of-prizes. Thursday, March 19 Reykjavík Has Its Own Soundtrack: For one night only, Icelandic multi-instrumentalist, producer, and musician DJ Hermigervill invites Seattle audiences to experience an intimate listening party at Shibuya HiFi. The evening explores the songs that capture what it means to live and grow up in Iceland’s vibrant capital. Hermigervill opens his personal archive for an exclusive journey through Reykjavík’s musical identity, sharing rare recordings and the stories behind them. Presented in the spirit of Reykjavík Music City, the event celebrates a capital where the city helps shape the sound. 7:30 - 9:00 PM at Shibuya HiFi. Learn more at https://www.inspiredbyiceland.com/taste-of-iceland/seattle/reykjavik-soundtrack. Friday, March 20 Across Iceland: A Guided Cocktail Journey: During this two-hour cocktail class, storyteller and Icelandic guide Ragnar Bragason will lead guests through some of Iceland’s most stunning regions, sharing tales from the road and the places where Iceland Hotel Collection by BERJAYA welcomes travelers. At each stop, Icelandic mixologist, Arnór Gísli, will teach guests to create a signature cocktail inspired by the region's landscape, mood, and character. Three destinations. Three drinks. One unforgettable trip. 4:00 - 6:00 PM at Life on Mars. Learn more at https://www.inspiredbyiceland.com/taste-of-iceland/seattle/cocktail-journey. Northern Lights in Seattle: Learn about Iceland’s fascinating northern lights—which are expected to shine even brighter in 2026 and 2027—with astronomy and science educator, Sævar "Astro-Sævar" Helgi Bragason, hosted by Icelandia. Sævar will explain why Iceland is the perfect place to view the August 12, 2026, solar eclipse and recommend his favorite tours in Iceland. Guests will also be invited to experience Iceland through virtual reality, with a focus on Icelandia's Northern Lights tours, Glacier Hikes, and ATV tours. 6:00 - 7:30 PM at the National Nordic Museum. Learn more at https://www.inspiredbyiceland.com/taste-of-iceland/seattle/northern-lights. Iceland Airwaves Off-Venue: Enjoy a free concert curated by Iceland Airwaves, an award-winning music festival in Reykjavík, featuring RAKEL and Spacestation, with DJ Hermigervill spinning Icelandic tunes between sets. RAKEL’s intimate, atmospheric music combines voice and emotion into something quietly powerful. Spacestation blends shimmering rock and textured synths, creating a cosmic energy that feels both nostalgic and fresh. DJ Hermigervill is a multi-instrumentalist, producer, composer, and DJ. Doors open at 7:00 PM at Conor Byrne Co-op. Learn more at https://www.inspiredbyiceland.com/taste-of-iceland/seattle/iceland-airwaves-off-venue. Saturday, March 21 Draw Iceland’s Stories! Family Workshop with Illustrator Rán Flygenring: Join award-winning Icelandic storyteller and illustrator Rán Flygenring for a playful, inspiring family art workshop where imagination meets Icelandic nature and history. In this workshop, Rán invites children and parents alike to explore storytelling through drawing, doodling, and other creative activities. Families will learn about Iceland’s powerful landscapes, trailblazing leaders, and creative spirit. All ages are welcome, and no prior art experience is required—just curiosity, imagination, and a sense of fun! 11:00 AM - 2:00 PM at the National Nordic Museum. Sign up at https://www.inspiredbyiceland.com/taste-of-iceland/seattle/draw-icelands-stories for a chance to win a signed edition of one of Rán's illustrated children’s books. Iceland Unfiltered: Get ready for an evening of laughter hosted by Icelandair! At this one-night-only comedy event, two Icelanders are flying to Seattle to reveal the quirky truths, cultural oddities, and straight-up strange realities of Iceland. Comedians and storytellers Ragnar Bragason and Bergur Ebbi will share hilarious personal stories, offer wildly unqualified advice, and roast what tourists get wrong…every single time. Each guest will receive a complimentary cocktail infused with local flavors and Blue Lagoon Iceland will provide every guest with a skincare gift. Expect laughter and at least five conversation-starters for your next dinner party. Spoiler: You've definitely been pronouncing Reykjavík wrong. 4:00 - 6:00 PM at The Rabbit Box. Learn more at https://www.inspiredbyiceland.com/taste-of-iceland/seattle/iceland-unfiltered. Taste of Iceland was created to celebrate and introduce the traditions and culture of Iceland to North America. For more information on Taste of Iceland, please visit https://www.inspiredbyiceland.com/taste-of-iceland/seattle.

From SEAtoday - Arts & Culture

item.title: SOLD OUT!! Son Little CityFolk Tour 2026 w/ Tonina
item.description:
Friday, March 20, 2026 at 8:30 PM

Location: Tractor, 5213 Ballard Avenue NW, Seattle, WA

Son Littles live show brings his music into a different kind of clarity- raw, expressive, and always evolving. Whether solo or with a full band, he moves fluidly between deep grooves, stripped-down intimacy, and moments that feel completely improvised yet emotionally precise. Its a performance style shaped by instinct and connection, grounded in the richness of his voice and the depth of his songwriting.Over the past decade, hes cultivated a sound that draws from soul, blues, folk, hip-hop, and Rs not just a setlist - its an experience.Following a run through the UK and Europe supporting Larkin Poe, Son Little is back with a new wave of music and a fresh stretch of tour dates for winter and spring. His new album CITYFOLK is set for release in March 2026, with recent singles in orbit and be better offering a first look at whats to come - both onstage and beyond in the months ahead. This event is 21 and over. item.title: SAM Body + Mind: Activity Days
item.description:
Saturday, March 21, 2026 at 9 AM

Location: Olympic Sculpture Park, 2901 Western Ave, Seattle, WA

Experience a full spectrum of grounding, creative, and wellness-focused practices with SAM Body \+ Mind: Activity Days. Designed to rejuvenate both inner and outer worlds, each session offers a mix of mindful movement, quiet reflection, hands-on creativity, and holistic care. Participate in gentle Qigong to awaken your energy, enjoy replenishing moments with acupuncture, or settle into the calm of silent reading in a peaceful shared space. Explore your artistic side with natural dye and art-making workshops that nourish connections to yourself and the natural world. Come for one activity or stay for the full experience-each offering invites you to slow down, reconnect, and care for your body and mind. 8:30 am | Doors open 9-10 am | Tai Chi/Qigong With Sensei Jody Garcia 10 am-1 pm | Workshops Art workshop with NW Expressive and Creative Arts Institute Natural dyes workshop with Earth Corps Acupuncture with Dr. Yanzi Jiang, Dr. Milo Jackson, and Dr. Nikeeta Shah noon-2 pm | Musical performance Ambient Vinyl DJ set performed by DJ Raica 1-2 pm | Silent reading With West Seattle Book Club Food and drinks Food and beverages will be available for purchase from Shooby Doo Catering in the PACCAR Pavilion. Presenting Sponsor

From SEAtoday - Arts & Culture

item.title: Alaska Cruise & Travel Show
item.description:
Saturday, March 21, 2026 at 9 AM

Location: Bell Harbor International Conference Center, 2211 Alaskan Way, Seattle, WA

Event Overview Join us in Seattle for the Alaska Cruise & Travel Show and begin planning your voyage to one of the world's most extraordinary destinations. This immersive event brings together travel inspiration, cultural experiences, and exclusive cruise offers all in one place. Experience live music and theater presentations that celebrate Alaska's landscapes and heritage. Participate in hands-on activities including gold panning and axe throwing and meet with cruise specialists to explore itineraries and take advantage of limited-time booking offers. Attendees may enter for the chance to win one Alaska cruise for two and one of six $500 Holland America Line gift cards. Admission is free. Reserve your place at www.alaskatravelshow.com.

From SEAtoday - Arts & Culture

Event details

item.title: Beyond Mysticism: The Modern Northwest
item.description:
Saturday, March 21, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Seattle Art Museum, 1300 First Avenue, Seattle, WA

image: Drift No. 2, 1936, Malcolm M. Roberts, American, 1913-1990, tempera on board, 25 1/2 x 20 5/8 in., Seattle Art Museum, Eugene Fuller Memorial Collection, 37.103, © Malcolm M. Roberts, photo: Scott Leen. Inspired by the region’s natural beauty and rapidly changing city, this exhibition showcases artists who told the raw story of Seattle in the 20th century. By sharing the Pacific Northwest’s perspective through trending art styles—including Social Realism, Surrealism, and Abstract Expressionism—these artists offered a unique take on Modernist art and challenged the “mystic” label ascribed to their works. Spearheading the movement were local icons Mark Tobey, Kenneth Callahan, Guy Anderson, and Morris Graves—but the range and breadth of Pacific Northwest art goes beyond the “Big Four” of the Northwest School. Featuring more than 150 pieces, this exhibition showcases works that investigate the complexities of daily life in the region. Whether applying an ecocritical lens or producing dreamy and uncanny visuals, these artists expressed the duality of the natural world and city life to warn of the environmental and social impacts of industrialization. From Malcolm Roberts to Kenjiro Nomura, learn how Northwest artists applied their regional perspective to broader artistic developments to create a new form of American Modernism. The exhibition is accompanied by a fully illustrated catalogue authored by Theresa Papanikolas, SAM’s Ann M. Barwick Curator of American Art.

From SEAtoday - Arts & Culture

Event details

item.title: TADAIMA: I'm Home
item.description:
Saturday, March 21, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Museum of History and Industry, 860 Terry Ave N, Seattle, Seattle, WA

After more than 80 years, a treasured collection returns “home” to Seattle’s Japanese American community. In 1942 dozens of traditional Boys’ and Girls’ Day dolls were entrusted to Bailey Gatzert Elementary School by Japanese American families facing forced incarceration. Most were never reclaimed after the war. Artist Miya Sukune uses recent research in MOHAI’s collection, as well as interviews with Japanese American survivors and their descendants, to create an installation called “Tadaima,” meaning “I’m home.” TADAIMA: “I’m Home!” Bringing Seattle’s Girls’ and Boys’ Day Dolls Home reconnects the dolls with their traditional role of protecting and blessing children. It invites visitors to celebrate the resilience, memory, and enduring traditions of the Japanese American Community.

From SEAtoday - Arts & Culture

item.title: Beyond Mysticism: The Modern Northwest
item.description:
Sunday, March 22, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Seattle Art Museum, 1300 First Avenue, Seattle, WA

image: Drift No. 2, 1936, Malcolm M. Roberts, American, 1913-1990, tempera on board, 25 1/2 x 20 5/8 in., Seattle Art Museum, Eugene Fuller Memorial Collection, 37.103, © Malcolm M. Roberts, photo: Scott Leen. Inspired by the region’s natural beauty and rapidly changing city, this exhibition showcases artists who told the raw story of Seattle in the 20th century. By sharing the Pacific Northwest’s perspective through trending art styles—including Social Realism, Surrealism, and Abstract Expressionism—these artists offered a unique take on Modernist art and challenged the “mystic” label ascribed to their works. Spearheading the movement were local icons Mark Tobey, Kenneth Callahan, Guy Anderson, and Morris Graves—but the range and breadth of Pacific Northwest art goes beyond the “Big Four” of the Northwest School. Featuring more than 150 pieces, this exhibition showcases works that investigate the complexities of daily life in the region. Whether applying an ecocritical lens or producing dreamy and uncanny visuals, these artists expressed the duality of the natural world and city life to warn of the environmental and social impacts of industrialization. From Malcolm Roberts to Kenjiro Nomura, learn how Northwest artists applied their regional perspective to broader artistic developments to create a new form of American Modernism. The exhibition is accompanied by a fully illustrated catalogue authored by Theresa Papanikolas, SAM’s Ann M. Barwick Curator of American Art.

From SEAtoday - Arts & Culture

Event details

item.title: TADAIMA: I'm Home
item.description:
Sunday, March 22, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Museum of History and Industry, 860 Terry Ave N, Seattle, Seattle, WA

After more than 80 years, a treasured collection returns “home” to Seattle’s Japanese American community. In 1942 dozens of traditional Boys’ and Girls’ Day dolls were entrusted to Bailey Gatzert Elementary School by Japanese American families facing forced incarceration. Most were never reclaimed after the war. Artist Miya Sukune uses recent research in MOHAI’s collection, as well as interviews with Japanese American survivors and their descendants, to create an installation called “Tadaima,” meaning “I’m home.” TADAIMA: “I’m Home!” Bringing Seattle’s Girls’ and Boys’ Day Dolls Home reconnects the dolls with their traditional role of protecting and blessing children. It invites visitors to celebrate the resilience, memory, and enduring traditions of the Japanese American Community.

From SEAtoday - Arts & Culture

Event details

item.title: Samantha Yun Wall: What We Leave Behind
item.description:
Sunday, March 22, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Seattle Art Museum, 1300 First Avenue, Seattle, WA

Samantha Yun Wall’s new paintings use overlapping silhouettes of female figures as portals to unknown spaces and different temporal realms. Impetus for the new body of work is a Korean folk tale in which the Pasque flower is symbolic of a grandmother who passed away without the loving care of her grandchildren. It is a story of melancholy, loss, and remembrance. The delicate hair on the flower’s stem differentiates it, and the artist gives the plant a surreal aspect in some of her paintings, replacing the flower’s center with a watchful eye.

From SEAtoday - Arts & Culture

Event details

item.title: Anila Quayyum Agha: Geometry of Light
item.description:
Sunday, March 22, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Seattle Art Museum, 1300 First Avenue, Seattle, WA

Anila Quayyum Agha: Geometry of Light invites you to become part of the art. Agha, a Pakistani American artist, animates spaces with her large-scale sculptural installations. Suspended from the ceiling, Agha’s steel cubes are laser-cut with intricate designs that project geometric shadows onto the visitor. She draws on both the light and dark of her own life, using South Asian art practices to convey the gender discrimination she faced growing up as a young girl in Pakistan. An in-depth exploration of Islamic architecture, art, and identity, this is the first solo exhibition of a Pakistani American artist in SAM’s 90-year history. Agha is carving a name out for herself in the art world—visit the Seattle Asian Art Museum for an immersive and illuminating experience.

From SEAtoday - Arts & Culture

item.title: Young Dragon: A Bruce Lee Story
item.description:
Sunday, March 22, 2026 at 11 AM

Location: Seattle Children's Theatre, 201 Thomas Street, Seattle, WA

Before he was a legend, Bruce Lee was just a young man trying to figure it all out—right here in Seattle. This action-packed, soul-searching story follows the Young Dragon as he navigates a new country and finds his voice. Through movement, magic, and heart, we meet the human behind the icon—flawed, fierce, and full of possibility. Watch as Lee learns to clear his brain by mimicking the white noise of water and begins fusing martial arts with philosophy. From street fights in Hong Kong to teaching self-defense and chasing big dreams, Bruce’s journey reminds us that finding your way means first figuring out who you are. Come see how Seattle shaped him into a flexible, fluid, and flowing master—and how he reshaped the world.

From SEAtoday - Arts & Culture

Event details

item.title: Boren Banner Series: Camille Trautman
item.description:
Sunday, March 22, 2026 at 11 AM

Location: Frye Art Museum, 704 Terry Ave, Seattle, WA

Every photograph is a reminder that the act of framing is never neutral. In Camille Trautman’s first solo museum exhibition in their hometown, the Seattle-born Duwamish artist uses photography and video to challenge colonial narratives and counter Indigenous erasure. The exhibition presents selections from their ongoing series The North American LCD—spectral self-portraits staged in varied natural landscapes. Rooted in Trautman’s process of coming out as transgender, the images show the artist’s body partially obscured by large LCD screens. The series examines how both landscape photography and digital media can shape or distort cultural identity, often by promoting or denying visibility.

From SEAtoday - Arts & Culture

item.title: Susan Meiselas: Crossings
item.description:
Sunday, March 22, 2026 at 12 PM

Location: Photographic Center Northwest (PCNW), 900 12th Ave., Seattle, WA

First presented at the Art Institute of Chicago in 1990, this exhibition by Susan Meiselas intertwines documentary photographs of Central America during the 1970s and 1980s \(from Nicaragua and El Salvador\) along with panoramic images from the US/Mexico border taken in 1989. Additional photographs of the US/Mexico border taken in 2018 are also featured in a site-specific gallery installation. The exhibition invites viewers to see immigration in the context of economic and political crises in the region. Today, the work feels newly urgent. US immigration enforcement has expanded through militarization and constant surveillance, with leaders using fear-driven language to divide communities and legitimize brutal forms of control. These photographs ask us to look carefully, to recognize familiar patterns, and to hold on to our shared humanity across the globe. People move in search of safety, possibility, and a better life for their families, which is a universal hope that crosses borders, generations, and time.

From SEAtoday - Arts & Culture

item.description:

Location: Tractor, 5213 Ballard Avenue NW, Seattle, WA

Music lovers and Lucinda Williams fans are invited to an inspiring Matinee event that blends live performance, documentary screening, and conversation as Finding Lucinda comes to The Tractor Tavern. This show celebrates the early life, creative spark, and lasting influence of the legendary singer-songwriter Lucinda Williams. To start the event, ISMAY and special guests will be performing Lucinda Williams songs.Finding Lucinda follows singer-songwriter ISMAY (Avery Hellman) on a transformative road trip through the American South, exploring the early life and enduring legacy of three-time GRAMMY-winning artist Lucinda Williams. Along the way, the film features never-before-seen footage, rare recordings, and interviews with Charlie Sexton, Buddy Miller, Mary Gauthier, and Lucinda herself.www.findinglucinda.com This event is 21 and over. item.title: EMBODIED ALTARS DANCE CLASS
item.description:
Monday, March 23, 2026 at 6:30 PM

Location: Daybreak Star Indian Cultural Center, 5011 Bernie Whitebear Way, Seattle, WA

EMBODIED ALTARS is an all-levels dance class hosted by Daybreak Star Radio. Designed by Indigenous Chicana artist and El Sueño director, Alicia Mullikin. Dancers will utilize Indigenous principles within movement, meditation, and song as a way to practice gratitude, honor the land, and recognize their ancestors. The class is for those who are interested in reflection, intention setting, and a discovery of their own stories. No dance experience necessary. Class Time: Mondays, 6:30-8PM No class 9/15/25 No class 10/13/25 Venue: Daybreak Star Indian Cultural Center 5011 Bernie Whitebear Way, Seattle, WA 98199 Sliding Scale: $10-$20\* per class. Sign Up Now!   This venue is ADA accessible w/ ADA bathrooms and a wheelchair lift to the second floor. Class will be held in the ballroom space, this area has 5 ADA parking spaces. There are an additional 25 parking spots in our lot, there is ample parking space along the road leading up to Daybreak star as well as the Discovery Park North Parking lot. What to bring: water, comfortable clothes to dance in, journal, your personal identity, a desire to explore new pathways and an open mind/heart. Hosted at Daybreak Star Indian Cultural Center Produced by United Indians of All Tribes Foundation In Partnership with El Sueno Dance & Daybreak Star Radio “Indigenize the Airwaves” “Art. Culture. Reclamation.” \*Talk to us about BIPOC and Queer/LGBTQIA2S\+ Scholorships\* Please contact @el.sueno.dance on IG for private conversations about this topic.

Volpone \(The Fox\), a wealthy Venetian, lusts for yet more gold, and with the help of his devious brilliant servant Mosca \(The Fly\), delights in finding ways to steal it from his wealthy, corrupt Venetian neighbors. Ben Jonson’s renowned 1606 comedy takes us on a wild funny ride with one of the greatest plots in all theatre through a greedy mad society where self-interest is the only real value, and deception the only truth. And where the greatest pleasure of all is…THE GRIFT.

item.title: Pearly Drops
item.description:
Monday, March 23, 2026 at 7 PM

Location: Neumos, 925 E Pike St, Seattle, WA

Barboza Presents The Voices Are Coming Back Tour PEARLY DROPS with Special Guests MON, 23 MAR 2026 at 07:00PM PDT Ages: 21 & Over Doors Open: 07:00PM OnSale: Fri, 29 Aug 2025 at 10:00AM PDT Announcement: Tue, 26 Aug 2025 at 10:00AM PDT Painting with a bold, slightly macabre palette, Pearly Drops’ Sandra Tervonen and Juuso Malin create a realm of strange, softly smudged pop. On the Helsinki act’s third full-length, The Voices Are Coming Back, fantasy and the mundane tangle into an alluring web of mystery. The record — issued by forward-thinking New York City label Music Website — centers on an age-old tale: the saga of Hollywood hopefuls. Branching beyond their home of Finland, Pearly Drops are an emerging fixture of the global indie-electronic scene. The act wraps sweet, dreamy melodies in futuristic hues. It’s fitting that Tervonen and Malin share backgrounds in music production and collaborated on sound design efforts before morphing into an artist project. Pearly Drops’ 2020 debut, Call For Help, and its 2023 follow up, A Little Disaster, introduced a mesmerizing formula — blending misty rock and electronic elements. The pair’s often playful, grungy fashion sense enhances these chic sonics, with all photos and styling handled in house. Pearly Drops has received a Teosto Prize — one of the Nordic countries’ most prestigious music awards. The duo has collaborated with artists such as Vitesse X and KROY, and their own songs have been covered and remixed by acts including XL Recordings' Nourished by Time and American dream-pop favorites Dream, Ivory. On The Voices Are Coming Back, Tervonen and Malin swapped the familiarity of Finland for the peculiar glamour of Los Angeles. “We'd always imagined LA through the lens of David Lynch: saturated in surrealism, a little menacing, a little magical — and that’s exactly how it felt. A puzzling, but beautiful cultural shock,” Tervonen and Malin reflect on the city. The bizarre energy of Southern California beams bright on album opener “Delusional On Sunset Boulevard.” “I think I just got older / Growing cold and dumber too / No pat on my shoulder / I ride my horse through Hollywood,” Tervonen’s elven vocals skate around vast chords and the strobe of a synth lead. Amid the sprawl, Pearly Drops encountered kindred spirits including Australian group Cub Sport and photographer Tatiana Bruening \(aka illumitati\), who were invited to contribute to The Voices Are Coming Back. True to their highly attentive ethos, Pearly Drops and these creative partners crafted the entire record themselves. "Sonically, we wanted the album to sound like a fictional reality, with vocals coming from, for example, a sewer and overheated analog modular synthesizers seamlessly blending with guitar-driven pop music,” Tervonen and Malin share. “We don’t identify as an indie rock band, but we don’t feel like an electronic act either. We composed, wrote, produced and mixed the entire album just the two of us with our own hands. Sandra has also painted all the cover arts by hand. For our artistic expression, it’s important to see through the whole process ourselves." Call For Help pondered loneliness and A Little Disaster grappled with death. The Voices Are Coming Back retains Pearly Drops’ signature introspection, though it stands as the duo’s most narrative full-length to date. “At its heart, this album is about the emotional unraveling that can happen when you chase a dream too hard—when fantasy and ambition start to bleed into your sense of identity. We used the idea of the “Hollywood dream” as a narrative frame, but really, it’s a metaphor for the deeper psychological shifts that come with longing and disillusionment. It’s about getting lost—not just in a place, but in yourself. Coming from Helsinki, there's always been a sense of distance or introspection in our music. This time, we wanted to embrace that and stretch it into something even more surreal and emotionally complex. The album isn’t just storytelling—it’s autofiction. The characters, the landscapes, the dream logic—they’re all extensions of our personal experiences as artists navigating uncertainty, burnout, and ambition in a world that’s constantly shifting.” The album draws a relatable, yet otherworldly picture adrift in uncertainty, drawn by the age-old allure of reinvention. Instead of glamor, they find themselves lost in the woods of Franklin Hills. Hiding behind a tree from mystical voices one moment, the next they’ve transformed into mermaids in the sewers under Sunset Boulevard. Time melts away on drives around the eastern Santa Monica Mountains. In the end, the only way back is through a forest in Silver Lake. Despite the storybook universe it conjures, Pearly Drops cite The Voices Are Coming Back as a work of autofiction. Still, grounded influences are present on the record, themes of anguish lingering beneath the surface. This pain is conveyed through voice memos from Bruening on the songs “Shallow,” “Demonlover,” and “Cocoon & Tatiana’s Lament.” Meanwhile, Cub Sport’s ethereal opera defines "Mermaid." “Laying in my mess / Mermaid in her manhole / It’s gone bad / I’m drinking the poison / I am seeing visions while awake,” Tervonen breathily sings in the first verse of the song over a thicket of chorus-heavy fretwork. “Ratgirl” continues “Mermaid”’s ride down Talmadge Street, inspired by fantasy, haunted by the echo of a promise that never arrived and made gripping by a dancey, electroclash pulse. “End Credits” serves as a spiritual intermission, Tervonen singing about a screen fading to black over a new wave instrumental. Late album highlight, “Pillow Face,” is a muffled scream about the dread of beauty. From start to finish, The Voices Are Coming Back is united by upbeat rhythms and gossamer textures. As The Voices Are Coming Back’s journey nears a close, Pearly Drops are emotionally unraveled. “Only believe in / My digital feelings / Still reaching for the / Undo in the void,” Tervonen sings on the closing track, “Silver Lake Mystery Forest.” “It feels like with this third album, we bottled up that feeling of losing your mind and your identity when trying to chase your dreams — whatever they happen to be,” Tervonen and Malin muse. Across this feverish vision of a record, the uncertainty and burnout of everyday life manifest in uncanny, albeit deeply human ways. Accessibility Disclaimer: Barboza is not wheelchair accessible. Minors will not be permitted entry. \* Doors open at the listed event time. \* Event start time is generally one hour after doors open but is subject to change without notice. \* All tickets are nonrefundable with the exception of event cancellation. \* Support acts are subject to change. \* Orders in violation of the published ticket limit are subject to cancellation without notice.

item.title: TADAIMA: I'm Home
item.description:
Tuesday, March 24, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Museum of History and Industry, 860 Terry Ave N, Seattle, Seattle, WA

After more than 80 years, a treasured collection returns “home” to Seattle’s Japanese American community. In 1942 dozens of traditional Boys’ and Girls’ Day dolls were entrusted to Bailey Gatzert Elementary School by Japanese American families facing forced incarceration. Most were never reclaimed after the war. Artist Miya Sukune uses recent research in MOHAI’s collection, as well as interviews with Japanese American survivors and their descendants, to create an installation called “Tadaima,” meaning “I’m home.” TADAIMA: “I’m Home!” Bringing Seattle’s Girls’ and Boys’ Day Dolls Home reconnects the dolls with their traditional role of protecting and blessing children. It invites visitors to celebrate the resilience, memory, and enduring traditions of the Japanese American Community.

item.title: MOHAI Mercantile Literary Series - Seattle's Locks and Ship Canal
item.description:
Tuesday, March 24, 2026 at 6 PM

Location: Museum of History and Industry, 860 Terry Ave N, Seattle, Seattle, WA

Register ➞ https://bit.ly/3NbLLJX MOHAI Mercantile is proud to host the official book launch event for Seattle’s Locks and Ship Canal with local historians David B. Williams and Jennifer Ott! This portable guide from UW Press and HistoryLink is an introduction to the engineering marvel of the Ballard locks, and a primer on the history of the ship canal. Hear from the authors about the tumultuous history of one of Seattle’s defining projects, and the dramatic changes it brought to the city’s economy, neighborhoods, and natural environment. Doors open at 6 pm for music and mingling. Food and drink available for purchase. Conversation and Q&A begin at 7 pm, followed by a book signing with the authors.

Join us every Tuesday night for **The Roadhouse Jazz Jam**, where the city’s best musicians — and brave newcomers — come together to celebrate the spirit of live jazz. Bring your instrument, your voice, or just your love for great music. The night starts with a house band set, then the stage opens up for an unforgettable jam session filled with swing, soul, and spontaneity. 🎵 Smooth vibes • 🎺 Live improvisation Come play, listen, and feel the groove — the Tuesday Jazz Jam is where the magic happens.

item.title: IMPROV 100 TUESDAY - Intro to Improv: Say Yes!, SPRING'26
item.description:
Tuesday, March 24, 2026 at 7 PM

Location: Unexpected Productions, 1428 Post Alley, Seattle, WA

Say yes! Learn to listen, connect, play and use your own life to create stories with others! An 8 week intro to improvised theatre! Discover Unexpected Productions' philosophy of improvisation, developed over our 43 years of improvising in Seattle! You'll learn how to actively listen, to use your own life to create collaborative stories, and you'll work on the fundamental principles of improvisation: what it means to say, "Yes, And", how to tell stories that include everyone in the room, how to stop worrying about failing, and the games and concepts behind what we do on stages around the world! Under the guidance of one of our best Seattle improv teachers, you will learn to:· -Be a Better Listener -Conquer Fears -Spark Creativity -Meet New People & Make New Friends -Become a Better Problem Solver -Gain Confidence· -Fail With Grace \(and Maybe Even Enjoy It\) -Say Yes More in Your Life -Get Out of Your Comfort Zone -Become a Better Public Speaker -Discover More About Yourself -...and have a blast doing it! Time and Dates : Tuesdays for eight \(8\) weeks, from March 24 to May 22, 7pm to 9:30pm NOTE: Attendance at the first session of your course is mandatory as it sets the agenda for the entire quarter. If you think you will have to miss the first class, please consider registering for the next quarter. No Prerequisites. All students begin at this level, even those with existing improv experience. It’s a prerequisite to the other levels. Location: 5700 6th Ave St, Suite 109, Umbrella Room, Seattle, WA 98108 \(In Georgetown, south of downtown, with plenty of parking.\) Maximum Students: 16 Cost: Ticket price $295.00 Age: 18 and above \*\*Cancellation policy: If you must cancel before the first class, we can only offer credit towards the same level class next quarter. We are unable to offer refunds. If you cancel after the first class we cannot offer credit towards a future class. Again, we are unable to offer refunds.

Event details

item.title: Chuckle Bucket Tuesday @ Club Comedy Seattle (Open Mic)
item.description:
Tuesday, March 24, 2026 at 7 PM

Location: Club Comedy Seattle, 328 15th Ave E, Seattle, WA

Club Comedy Seattle hosts Chuckle Bucket Tuesday each week. The luck of the bucket rules the night. Comics drop their name in and hope to be drawn for one of the spots on the list to tell jokes. It's fun, fast-paced and will keep you laughing hard on your Tuesday night. Door 7:00PM/Show 7:30PM Admission: $7 \+ $0.35 City Of Seattle Admission Tax 21\+ With Valid ID REQUIRED for entry. NO REFUNDS. FYI: If you have a large party it's easier for us to seat you all together if you purchase all the tickets under one name. . Club Comedy Seattle FAQ Is there an age/ID requirement? Yes. We are strictly 21\+ and valid physical ID is REQUIRED For Entry Is Seating First Come/First Served? Yes. The room is all two top tables and we seat guests in the order in which they arrive. Can I reserve a table for a group? We do not take reservations for tables, but groups can arrive early to secure seating together. Are tickets available at the door if an event is sold out online? If an event is sold out online there are no tickets available at the door. What are the club’s hours of operation? The club is open Tuesday to Sunday. Doors open a half hour before each show. What is the Parking like? Generally there is street parking available in the blocks surrounding the club. There are also affordable parking lots on 16th Ave E right around the block from the club along with other easy options in the neighborhood Is there a dress code? There is no formal dress code, but we recommend smart casual attire. Can I bring food or drinks? Outside food and drinks are not allowed. We have a full bar and a menu available for your enjoyment. What if I can't make it and need to cancel my ticket? All ticket sales are final. However, if you can’t make it, please contact us through Eventbrite in advance, and we’ll do our best to accommodate you. Will there be an opener for the headliner? Yes, headliner shows feature a lineup of comedians, including a host, an opener, and the headliner. Will the comics talk to me like I see in online videos all the time? Most likely they won't. Crowd work videos are popular for posting online, but crowd work doesn't generally happen at the online frequency in live comedy show situations. Some comics do engage the crowd as part of their regular set and it's generally always fun and more about making fun of themselves than making fun of the crowd. How can I get on the Cheap Laughs Open Mic On Wednesdays? Submissions for spots on the Wednesday Cheap Laughs Open Mic are accepted only on the Tuesday before between 12:00AM and 11:59PM via email at openmic@clubcomedyseattle.com Do you offer group discounts? Yes, we offer discounts for groups of ten or more. Please contact us for more details. Can I meet the comedians after the show? Meet-and-greet opportunities vary by event. Please ask staff for details after the show.

From SEAtoday - Arts & Culture

item.title: Beyond Mysticism: The Modern Northwest
item.description:
Wednesday, March 25, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Seattle Art Museum, 1300 First Avenue, Seattle, WA

image: Drift No. 2, 1936, Malcolm M. Roberts, American, 1913-1990, tempera on board, 25 1/2 x 20 5/8 in., Seattle Art Museum, Eugene Fuller Memorial Collection, 37.103, © Malcolm M. Roberts, photo: Scott Leen. Inspired by the region’s natural beauty and rapidly changing city, this exhibition showcases artists who told the raw story of Seattle in the 20th century. By sharing the Pacific Northwest’s perspective through trending art styles—including Social Realism, Surrealism, and Abstract Expressionism—these artists offered a unique take on Modernist art and challenged the “mystic” label ascribed to their works. Spearheading the movement were local icons Mark Tobey, Kenneth Callahan, Guy Anderson, and Morris Graves—but the range and breadth of Pacific Northwest art goes beyond the “Big Four” of the Northwest School. Featuring more than 150 pieces, this exhibition showcases works that investigate the complexities of daily life in the region. Whether applying an ecocritical lens or producing dreamy and uncanny visuals, these artists expressed the duality of the natural world and city life to warn of the environmental and social impacts of industrialization. From Malcolm Roberts to Kenjiro Nomura, learn how Northwest artists applied their regional perspective to broader artistic developments to create a new form of American Modernism. The exhibition is accompanied by a fully illustrated catalogue authored by Theresa Papanikolas, SAM’s Ann M. Barwick Curator of American Art.

From SEAtoday - Arts & Culture

item.title: TADAIMA: I'm Home
item.description:
Wednesday, March 25, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Museum of History and Industry, 860 Terry Ave N, Seattle, Seattle, WA

After more than 80 years, a treasured collection returns “home” to Seattle’s Japanese American community. In 1942 dozens of traditional Boys’ and Girls’ Day dolls were entrusted to Bailey Gatzert Elementary School by Japanese American families facing forced incarceration. Most were never reclaimed after the war. Artist Miya Sukune uses recent research in MOHAI’s collection, as well as interviews with Japanese American survivors and their descendants, to create an installation called “Tadaima,” meaning “I’m home.” TADAIMA: “I’m Home!” Bringing Seattle’s Girls’ and Boys’ Day Dolls Home reconnects the dolls with their traditional role of protecting and blessing children. It invites visitors to celebrate the resilience, memory, and enduring traditions of the Japanese American Community.

From SEAtoday - Arts & Culture

Event details

item.title: Samantha Yun Wall: What We Leave Behind
item.description:
Wednesday, March 25, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Seattle Art Museum, 1300 First Avenue, Seattle, WA

Samantha Yun Wall’s new paintings use overlapping silhouettes of female figures as portals to unknown spaces and different temporal realms. Impetus for the new body of work is a Korean folk tale in which the Pasque flower is symbolic of a grandmother who passed away without the loving care of her grandchildren. It is a story of melancholy, loss, and remembrance. The delicate hair on the flower’s stem differentiates it, and the artist gives the plant a surreal aspect in some of her paintings, replacing the flower’s center with a watchful eye.

From SEAtoday - Arts & Culture

Event details

item.title: Anila Quayyum Agha: Geometry of Light
item.description:
Wednesday, March 25, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Seattle Art Museum, 1300 First Avenue, Seattle, WA

Anila Quayyum Agha: Geometry of Light invites you to become part of the art. Agha, a Pakistani American artist, animates spaces with her large-scale sculptural installations. Suspended from the ceiling, Agha’s steel cubes are laser-cut with intricate designs that project geometric shadows onto the visitor. She draws on both the light and dark of her own life, using South Asian art practices to convey the gender discrimination she faced growing up as a young girl in Pakistan. An in-depth exploration of Islamic architecture, art, and identity, this is the first solo exhibition of a Pakistani American artist in SAM’s 90-year history. Agha is carving a name out for herself in the art world—visit the Seattle Asian Art Museum for an immersive and illuminating experience.

From SEAtoday - Arts & Culture

Event details

item.title: Boren Banner Series: Camille Trautman
item.description:
Wednesday, March 25, 2026 at 11 AM

Location: Frye Art Museum, 704 Terry Ave, Seattle, WA

Every photograph is a reminder that the act of framing is never neutral. In Camille Trautman’s first solo museum exhibition in their hometown, the Seattle-born Duwamish artist uses photography and video to challenge colonial narratives and counter Indigenous erasure. The exhibition presents selections from their ongoing series The North American LCD—spectral self-portraits staged in varied natural landscapes. Rooted in Trautman’s process of coming out as transgender, the images show the artist’s body partially obscured by large LCD screens. The series examines how both landscape photography and digital media can shape or distort cultural identity, often by promoting or denying visibility.

From SEAtoday - Arts & Culture

item.title: SY 101: Intro to Synthesis
item.description:
Wednesday, March 25, 2026 at 6 PM

Location: 305 Harrison Street, Seattle, Washington 98109, United States

Interested in learning more about synthesizers and how to develop your own synthesized sounds In this informative 2-hour workshop, our instructor will provide you with a detailed breakdown of the history of synthesis, Audio Path, Modulation, Basic Subtractive Patching, and more! *The use of a laptop is required to participate in this class. You will need to download VCV (free on laptops). Vera has a limited amount of laptops that we can provide on a first-come-first-serve basis to our members. If you do have your own laptop, we would appreciate if you used your own, so we can provide laptops to those who may not have one. If you would like to request a laptop, please email yewon.k@theveraproject.org. You will also need to download a couple files for this workshop. These files will be sent 24 hours before the class.

From The Vera Project

Event details

item.title: LS 101: Basics of Live Sound
item.description:
Wednesday, March 25, 2026 at 6 PM

Location: 305 Harrison Street, Seattle, Washington 98109, United States

Basics of Live Sound is a 2-hour introduction to live audio engineering and an orientation to Vera Project’s methods and equipment. Topics covered include an introduction to microphones and mic placement, signal paths, stands, cables, DI’s, snake, sub-snakes and etiquette for working with bands. This class is designed for both the novices and experts of all ages and will give an overview of signal flow and setting up small PAs, in addition to the basic requirements for being part of the Vera sound team at shows. Discover why the accommodating, open minded “Vera Attitude” of show production is appreciated worldwide! This class is a prerequisite for advanced audio classes at Vera and Sound Runner shifts at Vera Project show. Each Live Sound class unlocks new audio engineering volunteer shifts at Vera and eventually paid opportunities in and outside of the venue. Become a volunteer at Vera to access other audio-related educational programs such as tours, field trips, and internships!

From The Vera Project

Event details

item.title: SP 101: Intro to Vera's Screen Print Studio
item.description:
Wednesday, March 25, 2026 at 6 PM

Location: 305 Harrison Street, Seattle, Washington 98109, United States

This 2-hour, comprehensive hands-on class teaches you all the basics of screen printing at Vera’s shop: From coating and burning a screen, to pulling your very first print! Get comfortable with Vera's shop layout, equipment, and culture. Learn about techniques for creating screens, terminology, and more to create 1-color projects in the print shop. Students will walk away with the basic skills for screen printing 1-color paper prints, and their own print of a special Vera design, specific to the 101 class. This class is a prerequisite for other screenprint classes at Vera and access to Open Sessions in Vera's studio. Open Sessions are moderated by members of Vera's Screenprint Committee, and can provide guidance to participants on their projects. Open Sessions are scheduled for three times a month in 4 hour intervals. Participants must pay and register for an Open Session in advance on Vera's website.

From The Vera Project

item.title: Liz Cooper w/ Wes Parker
item.description:
Wednesday, March 25, 2026 at 8 PM

Location: 5433 Ballard Avenue Northwest, Seattle, Washington 98107, United States

Advance: $20 ($26.27 after fees) Day of: $25 ($31.42 after fees) Please note, the Sunset Tavern is a strictly 21+ bar and venue and does not accept vertical ID’s for entry. Liz Cooper: The thing about finding yourself is there's always another corner to turn. The Vermont-based singer/songwriter Liz Cooper made her third album during a period of intense self-discovery and reinvention. She moved to New York for the first time, weathered a pandemic, came out to herself after falling in love with a friend, and experienced her first queer relationship and breakup, all in the course of a few years. New Day marks both a personal and a musical revolution for Cooper – a plunge into psychedelic pop depths and a fullhearted reflection of a whirlwind chapter in her life. These songs scintillate with the kind of self-confidence that only beams through after you've aimed a sharp gaze inward and realized that whatever you see will always keep darting ahead of you.

From Sunset Tavern

item.title: Drop-In Studio
item.description:
Thursday, March 26, 2026 at 5 PM

Location: Frye Art Museum, 704 Terry Ave, Seattle, WA

Experiment freely, follow your curiosity, and create art at your own pace within a supportive community. Join us in the Frye Studio every fourth Thursday, where teaching artists will offer gentle guidance as you investigate provided materials and draw inspiration from the exhibitions on view. Come solo or with a friend; stay for fifteen minutes or settle in for the full session—the choice is yours. All skill levels are welcome, and youth may participate with an accompanying adult. Materials are included.

item.title: Jazz Month Tour
item.description:
Friday, March 27, 2026 at 7:30 AM

Location: The Royal Room, 5000 Rainier Ave South, Seattle, WA

March’s Women in Jazz Month comes alive as award-winning singer-songwriter Eugenie Jones lights up the Royal Room with her national Women in Jazz Month Tour. With stops in Denver, San Francisco, Atlanta, and Seattle, this tour joins a worldwide tribute to the women who shaped jazz, honoring trailblazers such as Billie Holiday, Nina Simone, and Ella Fitzgerald. At the Royal Room, Jones will blend beloved songs from female jazz legends, her own Jazz Week \#4 ranked originals, imaginative spins on vintage jazz, and timeless rhythm and blues classics. She’ll be joined by the remarkable talents of pianist Peter Adams, bassist Osama Afifi, and drummer Maria Wulff. As a Seattle Jazz Hall of Fame inductee, the first vocal recording artist to receive Earshot Jazz’s Album of the Year award, a two-time Vocalist of the Year winner, and a Jazz Journalists Association’s Jazz Hero Award winner, Jones has received high praise for her vocal prowess. Paris Move magazine describes Jones as “One of the most beautiful voices in the United States; a consummate interpreter who slips effortlessly into the soul of her songs.” Preview Jones’ music, watch videos, or learn more about this gifted entertainer at https://ffm.to/eugeniejonesmusic. Get your tickets today for this unforgettable night of exquisite vocals, masterful musicianship, and stirring storytelling, all in tribute to the enduring legacy and vibrant contributions of women in jazz. Tickets: https://www.strangertickets.com/events/180924339/eugenie-jones

From SEAtoday - Arts & Culture
